html:
[807]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明日 - Literally 'the next day,' used to indicate a specific day following the current one.
  • 向老吴戏谑曰 - '向' means 'to' or 'toward,' '老吴' refers to an elderly man named Wu, and '戏谑' means 'to jest' or 'to tease.'
  • 大快活 - Colloquial expression meaning 'very happy' or 'extremely pleased.'
  • 屡言之 - Means 'repeatedly said' or 'kept mentioning.'
  • 究问所以 - Means 'asked the reason' or 'inquired about the cause.'
  • 目击 - Literally 'seen with one's own eyes,' used to emphasize personal observation.
  • 抵赖乎 - Means 'to deny' or 'to refuse to admit.'
  • 爽然失色 - Phrase meaning 'to look startled' or 'to appear shocked.'
  • 至亲 - Means 'close relatives' or 'immediate family members.'
  • 亡去已十数年 - Means 'has been gone for more than ten years.'
  • 不识何故寻我 - Means 'I don't know why they are looking for me.'
  • 惊异 - Means 'astonished' or 'surprised.'
  • 颜色沮丧 - Means 'looked dejected' or 'appeared depressed.'
  • 至晚 - Means 'by evening' or 'by nightfall.'
  • 默默无语 - Means 'silent and speechless.'
  • 畏惧之容愈甚 - Means 'looked even more fearful.'
  • 拉小吴至房同居 - Means 'took Xiao Wu to the room to stay together.'
  • 力辞 - Means 'strongly refused.'
  • 不得已 - Means 'had no choice' or 'was forced to.'
  • 命二仆夹床而卧 - Means 'ordered two servants to lie beside the bed.'
  • 彻夜潜听 - Means 'listened intently all night.'
  • 毫无声息 - Means 'no sound at all.'
  • 至晓 - Means 'by morning' or 'at dawn.'
  • 已死矣 - Means 'was already dead.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 老吴 - Refers to an elderly man named Wu, a common name in Chinese culture.
  • 小吴 - Refers to a younger man named Wu, likely a relative or friend of the elderly Wu.
  • 汪二姑娘 - Refers to a young woman named Wang Er, a common name in Chinese culture.
  • 绍兴吴三 - Refers to a man named Wu San from Shaoxing, a city in Zhejiang Province known for its cultural significance.

Proper Nouns

  • 老吴 - An elderly man named Wu.
  • 小吴 - A younger man named Wu.
  • 汪二姑娘 - A young woman named Wang Er.
  • 绍兴吴三 - A man named Wu San from Shaoxing.

[808]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • ○ - A traditional Chinese punctuation mark used to introduce a new section or paragraph.
  • 舊在唐颓山 - Means 'originally located on Tang颓 Mountain.'
  • 国初迁于北城外阳彭山 - Means 'moved to Yangpeng Mountain outside the northern city wall at the beginning of the dynasty.'
  • 殿宇廊庑修洁 - Describes the temple's halls and corridors as clean and well-maintained.
  • 即丽春台古迹也 - Means 'which is the ancient site of Lichun Terrace.'
  • 地近孔道 - Means 'near a major thoroughfare.'
  • 绅当道迎送饮饯 - Describes the practice of officials and scholars welcoming and sending off guests with drinks and banquets.
  • 自城门迁后 - Means 'after the city gate was moved.'
  • 路既隔远 - Means 'the road became distant.'
  • 此寺遂废 - Means 'this temple was thus abandoned.'
  • 惟存大铜佛三尊 - Means 'only three large copper statues remained.'
  • 相传五代时所铸 - Means 'it is said they were cast during the Five Dynasties period.'
  • 约数万斤 - Means 'weighing about tens of thousands of pounds.'
  • 露处山内 - Means 'exposed in the mountain.'
  • 潜勾通书役销熔而朋分之 - Means 'secretly conspired with the book clerks to melt them down and divide the proceeds.'
  • 议定工费皆谢出 - Means 'agreed that all costs would be covered by Xie.'
  • 谢取其半，诸人分其半 - Means 'Xie took half, and the others divided the rest.'
  • 销毁之日，四体皆化 - Means 'on the day of destruction, all parts melted.'
  • 惟佛头不坏 - Means 'only the Buddha's head remained intact.'
  • 众皆疑惧 - Means 'everyone was suspicious and fearful.'
  • 谢曰：“此易事耳。” - Means 'Xie said, 'This is easy.''
  • 登炉溺之 - Means 'he climbed into the furnace and drowned it.'
  • 佛头竟毁 - Means 'the Buddha's head was finally destroyed.'
  • 谢年四十余，尚无子 - Means 'Xie was in his forties and still childless.'
  • 是时方欢笑间 - Means 'at that moment, while they were laughing.'
  • 佣工者至前，贺家中已生子矣 - Means 'a laborer came forward and congratulated him, saying his home had already given birth to a son.'
  • 谢大喜，以为此佛劫数，当为我毁，遂名其子为“谢铜头” - Means 'Xie was very happy, thinking this was the Buddha's fate, and that it was meant to be destroyed for him, so he named his son 'Xie Tongtou'.'
  • 家由此少裕 - Means 'his family became somewhat prosperous.'
  • 日以私铸制钱为事 - Means 'he made a living by privately casting coins.'
  • 数年后，其党以私铸见获 - Means 'several years later, his accomplices were caught for private coinage.'
  • 词连谢某 - Means 'the case implicated Xie.'
  • 谢自以热灰揉瞎双目 - Means 'Xie rubbed his eyes blind with hot ash.'
  • 到案时，言目瞽已久，仇扳显然竟得漏网 - Means 'when he appeared in court, he claimed he had been blind for a long time, and the obvious enmity made it clear he escaped the net.'
  • 及铜头长成，仍事私铸 - Means 'when Tongtou grew up, he continued to cast coins privately.'
  • 复为人所控 - Means 'he was again reported.'
  • 乾隆某年，父子对缚，斩于阳彭山下 - Means 'in a certain year of the Qianlong reign, father and son were bound together and beheaded at Yangpeng Mountain.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐颓山 - Refers to Tang颓 Mountain, a specific location.
  • 国初 - Refers to the early years of a dynasty.
  • 北城外阳彭山 - Refers to Yangpeng Mountain outside the northern city wall.
  • 丽春台 - Refers to Lichun Terrace, an ancient site.
  • 孔道 - Refers to a major thoroughfare.
  • 绅 - Refers to officials and scholars.
  • 五代 - Refers to the Five Dynasties period (907-960 AD).
  • 乾隆 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Qianlong (1736-1795 AD).

Proper Nouns

  • 唐颓山 - Tang颓 Mountain.
  • 北城外阳彭山 - Yangpeng Mountain outside the northern city wall.
  • 丽春台 - Lichun Terrace.
  • 五代 - Five Dynasties period.
  • 乾隆 - Qianlong reign.

[809]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 烏頭太子胡某 - Proper name, possibly referring to a historical or legendary figure. '烏頭' (Wutou) means 'black head' or 'crow-headed,' and '太子' (Tai Zi) means 'prince' or 'crown prince.'
  • 世以丹徒江上洲田為業 - The family has made a living by farming the fields on the island in the river near Dantu. '世' (Shi) means 'generation after generation' or 'ancestral,' '以' (Yi) means 'by,' '為業' (Wei Ye) means 'make a living.'
  • 乾隆十八年冬初 - Early winter of the eighteenth year of the Qianlong era. '乾隆' (Qian Long) is the reign title of Emperor Qianlong, '十八年' (Shi Ba Nian) means 'eighteenth year,' and '冬初' (Dong Chu) means 'early winter.'
  • 至洲收租 - To go to the island to collect rent. '至' (Zhi) means 'to go,' '洲' (Zhou) means 'island,' '收租' (Shou Zu) means 'collect rent.'
  • 以所收稻晒於场上 - To dry the harvested rice on the field. '以' (Yi) means 'with,' '所收稻' (Suo Shou Dao) means 'harvested rice,' '晒於场上' (Shai Yu Chang Shang) means 'dry on the field.'
  • 有乌鸦群集食稻 - There were crows gathering to eat the rice. '有' (You) means 'there were,' '乌鸦' (Wu Ya) means 'crows,' '群集' (Qun Ji) means 'gathering,' '食稻' (Shi Dao) means 'eat rice.'
  • 吴取土块逐之 - Wu picked up a piece of soil and chased them. '吴' (Wu) is the name, '取' (Qu) means 'pick up,' '土块' (Tu Kuai) means 'piece of soil,' '逐之' (Zhu Zhi) means 'chase them.'
  • 随手中一乌 - One of them fell into his hand. '随手中' (Sui Shou Zhong) means 'into his hand,' '一乌' (Yi Wu) means 'one of them.'
  • 哑然坠地 - Fell silently to the ground. '哑然' (Ya Ran) means 'silently,' '坠地' (Zui Di) means 'fall to the ground.'
  • 复奋起飞去 - And flew away again. '复' (Fu) means 'again,' '奋起' (Fen Qi) means 'fly up,' '飞去' (Fei Qu) means 'fly away.'
  • 吴归庄房 - Wu returned to his house. '归' (Gui) means 'return,' '庄' (Zhuang) means 'house,' '房' (Fang) means 'house.'
  • 晚餐后 - After dinner. '晚餐' (Wan Can) means 'dinner,' '后' (Hou) means 'after.'
  • 启户仰视 - Opened the door and looked up. '启户' (Qi Hu) means 'open the door,' '仰视' (Yang Shi) means 'look up.'
  • 天色深黑 - The sky was very dark. '天色' (Tian Se) means 'sky color,' '深黑' (Shen Hei) means 'very dark.'
  • 大雨如注 - Heavy rain poured down. '大雨' (Da Yu) means 'heavy rain,' '如注' (Ru Zhu) means 'poured down.'
  • 急入室 - Hurriedly entered the room. '急' (Ji) means 'hurriedly,' '入室' (Ru Shi) means 'entered the room.'
  • 衣色全白 - His clothes were completely white. '衣色' (Yi Se) means 'clothes color,' '全白' (Quan Bai) means 'completely white.'
  • 皆鸦粪矣 - All covered in crow droppings. '皆' (Jie) means 'all,' '鸦粪' (Ya Fen) means 'crow droppings,' '矣' (Yi) means 'already.'
  • 禽粪着身者不吉 - Those who have bird droppings on their bodies are not auspicious. '禽粪' (Qin Fen) means 'bird droppings,' '着身' (Zhuo Shen) means 'on the body,' '不吉' (Bu Ji) means 'not auspicious.'
  • 我今被污 - I am now defiled. '我' (Wo) means 'I,' '今' (Jin) means 'now,' '被污' (Bei Wu) means 'defiled.'
  • 殆将死乎 - Am I going to die? '殆' (Dai) means 'perhaps,' '将死' (Jiang Si) means 'going to die,' '乎' (Hu) means 'am I.'
  • 自此遂病雀爪风 - From then on, he fell ill with the disease called 'sparrow claw wind.' '自此' (Zi Ci) means 'from then on,' '遂病' (Sui Bing) means 'fell ill,' '雀爪风' (Que Zhao Feng) means 'sparrow claw wind.'
  • 手足抽掣 - His hands and feet twitched. '手足' (Shou Zu) means 'hands and feet,' '抽掣' (Chou Che) means 'twitched.'
  • 不便起卧 - Unable to sit up or lie down. '不便' (Bu Bian) means 'unable,' '起卧' (Qi Wo) means 'sit up or lie down.'
  • 又不能持物饮食 - And could not hold objects or eat. '又' (You) means 'and,' '不能' (Bu Neng) means 'could not,' '持物' (Chi Wu) means 'hold objects,' '饮食' (Yin Shi) means 'eat.'
  • 需人扶喂 - Needed someone to help him eat. '需人' (Xu Ren) means 'needed someone,' '扶喂' (Fu Wei) means 'help him eat.'
  • 不堪其苦 - Could not bear the pain. '不堪' (Bu Kan) means 'could not bear,' '其苦' (Qi Ku) means 'the pain.'
  • 心甚明晰 - His mind was very clear. '心' (Xin) means 'mind,' '甚' (Shen) means 'very,' '明晰' (Ming Xi) means 'clear.'
  • 因自念鸦食我稻 - He thought to himself that the crows ate my rice. '因' (Yin) means 'because,' '自念' (Zi Nian) means 'thought to himself,' '鸦食' (Ya Shi) means 'crows ate,' '我稻' (Wo Dao) means 'my rice.'
  • 我逐之 - I chased them. '我' (Wo) means 'I,' '逐之' (Zhu Zhi) means 'chased them.'
  • 有何过 - What wrong have I done? '有何' (You He) means 'what,' '过' (Guo) means 'wrong.'
  • 乃敢祟我 - Dare to torment me? '乃' (Nai) means 'dare,' '敢' (Gan) means 'dare,' '祟我' (Su Wei) means 'torment me.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乾隆 - Reign title of Emperor Qianlong.
  • 丹徒 - A place name, likely referring to a region in present-day Jiangsu Province, China.
  • 雀爪风 - A disease mentioned in the text, possibly a folk illness or a specific condition.

Proper Nouns

  • 烏頭太子胡某 - Proper name, possibly referring to a historical or legendary figure.
  • 吳 - Name of the character in the story.
  • 雀爪風 - Name of the disease mentioned in the text.

[810]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 須臾 - Classical temporal expression meaning 'a short while' or 'momentarily.'
  • 空中又下黑云二片 - Vivid, almost mythic description of two dark clouds descending from the sky, signifying supernatural events.
  • 化作少年 - Transformation phrase indicating that the clouds turned into a young man, a common motif in folklore and mythology.
  • 玄色冠巾 - Describes the young man's attire, with '玄色' meaning 'dark blue' or 'black' and '冠巾' referring to a headgear, suggesting a scholarly or monastic appearance.
  • 持黑伞随其后 - Descriptive phrase indicating that the young man is followed by someone holding a black umbrella, adding to the mysterious and otherworldly atmosphere.
  • 君欲控乌头太子耶？ - Rhetorical question asking if the person (吴) wants to accuse the '乌头太子' (Utu Prince), where '控' means 'to accuse' or 'to sue.'
  • 控词何拟？ - Question asking what the accusation is based on, where '控词' means 'accusation' and '何拟' means 'what is it based on?'
  • 君前击中太子，故有此疾 - Statement explaining that the person (吴) previously struck the prince, which is the reason for the current illness.
  • 今知其误也，某为君缓颊于太子 - Statement indicating that the young man now knows the mistake was made and will intercede on behalf of the person (吴) with the prince.
  • 可保君如旧，何须控告耶？ - Rhetorical question suggesting that the young man can ensure the person (吴) returns to normal, so there is no need for an accusation.
  • 因取控词怀之飞去 - Descriptive phrase indicating that the young man takes the accusation, puts it in his chest, and flies away.
  • 遽前往夺 - Phrase indicating that the person (吳) immediately rushes to take back the accusation.
  • 忽然惊醒 - Phrase indicating that the person (吳) suddenly wakes up from a dream or trance.
  • 自此所患渐愈，两月后平复如常 - Statement indicating that the person's illness gradually improves and returns to normal after two months.
  • 吴生两入阴间 - Phrase indicating that the person (吳) has entered the underworld twice.
  • 丹徒旧家子也 - Phrase indicating that the person (吳) is from an old family in Dan Tu.
  • 其祖、父俱在庠序 - Statement indicating that the person's grandfather and father were both scholars.
  • 祖为人端直，乡闾推重，殁十数年 - Statement indicating that the person's grandfather was upright and respected by the community, and he died more than ten years ago.
  • 某始娶妇，琴瑟甚笃 - Statement indicating that the person (吳) recently married, and his relationship with his wife was very harmonious.
  • 乾隆丙子，其妇暴卒 - Statement indicating that the person's wife died suddenly in the year 1746 (Qianlong 11th year).
  • 吴追思不已 - Phrase indicating that the person (吳) could not stop missing his wife.
  • 朱长班者，合城皆知其走阴差 - Statement indicating that Zhu Changban is known throughout the city as a messenger of the underworld.
  • 因吴治丧，彼朝夕来供役 - Statement indicating that Zhu Changban came to help with the funeral arrangements day and night.
  • 吴因私问阴司事 - Phrase indicating that the person (吳) privately asked about matters in the underworld.
  • 阴阳道隔，生人尤不宜滥入 - Statement indicating that the boundary between the living and the dead is distinct, and it is especially inappropriate for the living to enter the underworld without reason.
  • 老相公侍我甚好，我岂肯作此狡狯 - Statement indicating that the elder (朱长班) treated him very well, and he would not do such a trick.
  • 此事我不为，相公果坚意欲往，可往城里太平桥侧寻丹阳常妈，许以重资，或可同往 - Statement indicating that if the person (吳) insists on going, he should go to the side of Tai Ping Bridge in the city and find Dan Yang Chang Ma, offering a substantial reward, and perhaps they can go together.
  • 吴欣然 - Phrase indicating that the person (吳) was very pleased.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 阴间 - Refers to the underworld, a concept in Chinese mythology and religion.
  • 朱长班 - A character who is a messenger of the underworld, a common figure in Chinese folklore.
  • 丹阳常妈 - A character who is a medium or spiritual guide, often found in traditional Chinese beliefs.
  • 乾隆丙子 - The year 1746, during the reign of Emperor Qianlong of the Qing Dynasty.
  • 庠序 - Refers to schools or educational institutions in ancient China.
  • 琴瑟甚笃 - A metaphor for a harmonious and loving relationship, often used to describe a good marriage.
  • 暴卒 - Refers to a sudden death, often implying an unexpected and untimely demise.

Proper Nouns

  • 吴 - The main character of the story.
  • 朱长班 - A messenger of the underworld.
  • 丹阳常妈 - A medium or spiritual guide.
  • 乌头太子 - A prince, possibly a fictional or mythological figure.
  • 丹徒 - A place name, likely referring to a region in China.
  • 乾隆丙子 - The year 1746, during the reign of Emperor Qianlong of the Qing Dynasty.
  • 庠序 - Schools or educational institutions in ancient China.

[811]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 次日 - Temporal marker meaning 'the next day.'
  • 尋得 - Found or tracked down, implying a search.
  • 初亦不允 - Initially refused, indicating a change in attitude.
  • 許錢数千 - Offered several thousand coins, showing a bribe or payment.
  • 始允之 - Finally agreed, marking a turning point.
  • 相公某日可择一静屋独宿 - You may choose a quiet room to stay alone on a certain day, a specific instruction.
  • 我即来相约 - I will come to meet you, indicating a future action.
  • 衣履一切，不可使人稍为移动 - All clothing and footwear must not be moved by anyone, a strict requirement.
  • 稍移动，即不能还阳矣 - If moved even slightly, you will not return to life, a superstitious warning.
  • 谆嘱再四而归 - After repeatedly urging, I returned, indicating persistence.
  • 妻殁后 - After my wife's death, marking a significant event.
  • 独宿于一厢屋内 - Stay alone in a side room, a specific action.
  • 私嘱其婶母曰 - Privately instructed his aunt, indicating a confidential communication.
  • 侄今病甚，须早卧 - My nephew is very ill and must go to bed early, a medical concern.
  • 望婶母为我锁房 - Please lock the room for me, a request for security.
  • 切不可令人擅入动我衣履 - Absolutely do not let anyone enter or move my clothes and shoes, a strict prohibition.
  • 此侄生死关头也 - This is a critical moment for my nephew, emphasizing the gravity of the situation.
  • 甚骇 - Very frightened, indicating a strong emotional response.
  • 问其故，不告 - Asked the reason, but he did not tell, indicating secrecy.
  • 乃阴为检点之 - So secretly checked on him, indicating a covert action.
  • 然一灯于床前 - Lit a lamp in front of the bed, a specific action.
  • 心有此事，展转不寝 - Having this matter on his mind, he tossed and turned without sleeping, indicating anxiety.
  • 私念曰 - Thought to himself, indicating internal monologue.
  • 彼原未嘱我熟睡 - He originally did not tell me to sleep deeply, indicating a lack of explicit instruction.
  • 但彼从何来招我耶 - But how could he come to summon me? indicating skepticism.
  • 抑妄言耶 - Or is it just idle talk? indicating doubt.
  • 二鼓后 - After the second drumbeat, indicating a specific time.
  • 见有黑烟一线自窗隙间入 - Saw a thin line of black smoke enter through the window crack, describing a supernatural event.
  • 袅袅然如蛇之吐舌也 - Winding like a snake's tongue, a vivid description.
  • 心甚惧 - Feeling very afraid, indicating a strong emotional response.
  • 少顷 - A short while later, indicating a passage of time.
  • 其烟变成一黑团 - The smoke turned into a black ball, describing a transformation.
  • 大如斗 - As big as a winnowing basket, indicating size.
  • 直扑吴面 - Directly hit Wu's face, indicating a sudden action.
  • 遂昏晕 - And then he fainted, indicating a loss of consciousness.
  • 有人在耳边悄言曰 - Someone whispered in his ear, indicating a mysterious voice.
  • 吴相公同去 - Come with me, Scholar Wu, indicating an invitation.
  • 声即常妪也 - The voice was that of the old woman, identifying the speaker.
  • 以手扶起 - Helped him up, indicating assistance.
  • 同由门隙而出 - Together they exited through the door crack, indicating a specific action.
  • 所过窗户皆无碍 - All the windows and doors they passed through were unobstructed, indicating ease of movement.
  • 见其婶母房门有火光数丛 - Saw several clusters of firelight at his aunt's room door, describing a scene.
  • 盖与诸弟同宿于内 - It was where his brothers were staying together, indicating a location.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 相公 - A respectful title for a scholar or gentleman, often used in the context of a family or social setting.
  • 婶母 - Aunt, indicating a familial relationship.
  • 静屋 - Quiet room, a place for solitude and reflection.
  • 衣履 - Clothing and footwear, emphasizing personal belongings.
  • 还阳 - Return to life, a superstitious belief in the afterlife.
  • 二鼓后 - After the second drumbeat, indicating a specific time in the night, traditionally used for timekeeping in ancient China.
  • 黑烟一线 - A thin line of black smoke, often associated with supernatural or ghostly phenomena in Chinese folklore.
  • 蛇之吐舌 - A snake's tongue, a common metaphor for something winding or coiling.
  • 昏晕 - Faint, indicating a loss of consciousness, often associated with supernatural or ghostly encounters.

Proper Nouns

  • 常妈 - Old woman, a specific character in the story.
  • 吴 - Wu, the protagonist of the story.
  • 婶母 - Aunt, a specific character in the story.
  • 诸弟 - Brothers, indicating a familial relationship.

[812]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 甫出大门 - 甫 means 'just' or 'immediately,' and 出大门 means 'to step out of the main gate.' Together, it conveys the idea of 'just stepping out of the main gate.'
  • 黄沙漫漫 - A vivid description of a vast expanse of yellow sand, emphasizing the desolate and boundless nature of the landscape.
  • 不辨南北 - Literally 'cannot distinguish north and south,' indicating complete disorientation or confusion about one's surroundings.
  • 与人世仿佛 - 仿佛 means 'resembling' or 'similar to,' suggesting that the street market and government offices seen along the way are reminiscent of those in the human world.
  • 此即佛家所谓‘血污池’也 - 即 means 'is,' and 所谓 means 'so-called' or 'as called.' This phrase introduces the concept of the 'Blood Stained Pool' as understood in Buddhist teachings.
  • 娘子想在其内 - 娘子 is a respectful term for a woman, and 想在其内 means 'likely to be inside.' This suggests that the woman might be in the pool.
  • 吴左右顾 - 左右顾 means 'looked around,' indicating that Wu looked in all directions.
  • 垂泪与语 - 垂泪 means 'tears falling,' and 与语 means 'to converse.' Together, it conveys the act of speaking while crying.
  • 以手来拉吴入池 - 以手来拉 means 'with her hand pulling,' and 入池 means 'into the pool.' This describes the woman reaching out to pull Wu into the pool.
  • 池水涓滴着人，即不能返 - 涓滴 means 'a drop,' and 着人 means 'touching someone.' This phrase indicates that even a drop of the pool water touching someone prevents them from returning.
  • 入此池者，皆由生平毒虐婢妾之故 - 皆由 means 'all due to,' and 生平毒虐婢妾之故 means 'the past cruel treatment of maids and concubines.' This explains that those who enter the pool did so because of their past mistreatment of maids and concubines.
  • 凡殴婢妾见血不止者 - 凡 means 'any,' 殴 means 'beat,' and 见血不止 means 'bleeding non-stop.' This specifies that anyone who beat a maid or concubine to the point of continuous bleeding would enter the pool.
  • 以婢妾身上流血之多寡为入池之浅深 - 以...为 means 'based on,' 多寡 means 'amount,' and 浅深 means 'shallow or deep.' This indicates that the depth of the pool entry is determined by the amount of blood shed.
  • 我娘子并无殴婢妾 - 并无 means 'not at all,' and 殴婢妾 means 'beat maids or concubines.' This asserts that Wu's wife did not beat any maids or concubines.
  • 此前生事也 - 此 means 'this,' 前生 means 'past life,' and 事 means 'matter.' This suggests that the matter pertains to a past life.
  • 娘子并未生产 - 并未 means 'not at all,' and 生产 means 'give birth.' This clarifies that Wu's wife has not given birth.
  • 生产是人间常事，有何罪过 - 常事 means 'common occurrence,' and 有何罪过 means 'what sin is there.' This emphasizes that giving birth is a common human event and not a sin.
  • 言毕，牵吴从原路归 - 言毕 means 'after saying,' 牵 means 'lead,' 从 means 'along,' 原路 means 'the original path,' and 归 means 'return.' This describes the old woman leading Wu back along the original path after her explanation.
  • 吴昏睡过午始起 - 昏睡 means 'deep sleep,' 过午 means 'after noon,' and 始起 means 'finally woke up.' This indicates that Wu slept deeply until after noon before waking up.
  • 面色黄白若久病者 - 面色 means 'face color,' 黄白 means 'yellowish-white,' 若 means 'as if,' and 久病者 means 'someone who has been ill for a long time.' This describes Wu's face color as resembling that of someone who has been ill for a long time.
  • 数日方复 - 数日 means 'several days,' and 方复 means 'finally recovered.' This indicates that it took several days for Wu to fully recover.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 佛家所谓‘血污池’ - Refers to the Buddhist concept of the 'Blood Stained Pool,' which is a place of punishment for those who have committed cruel acts, particularly against women.
  • 娘子 - A respectful term for a woman, often used in classical Chinese literature.
  • 吴 - The name of the male protagonist in the story.
  • 常妪 - The name of the old woman who guides Wu.
  • 血污池 - A place of punishment in Buddhist cosmology for those who have committed cruel acts, especially against women.
  • 生平毒虐婢妾 - Refers to the past cruel treatment of maids and concubines, a common theme in classical Chinese literature and moral teachings.
  • 殴婢妾见血不止 - Describes the act of beating a maid or concubine to the point of continuous bleeding, which is considered a grave sin in traditional Chinese moral values.
  • 前生事 - Refers to the concept of past lives and karma, which is central to Buddhist and some Confucian beliefs.
  • 生产 - Refers to childbirth, a common and natural human event in traditional Chinese society.
  • 人间常事 - Refers to common human events, emphasizing the normalcy of childbirth.

Proper Nouns

  • 吴 - The name of the male protagonist.
  • 常妪 - The name of the old woman who guides Wu.
  • 娘子 - A respectful term for a woman, often used in classical Chinese literature.
  • 血污池 - A place of punishment in Buddhist cosmology for those who have committed cruel acts, especially against women.

  • 遣舆夫送至河畔 - Means 'sent the sedan-chair bearers to take him to the riverbank.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 狐道学 - A satirical term combining 'fox' (associated with trickery or supernatural beings) with 'scholar,' describing the trapper as a scholar-like figure despite his occupation.
  • 巨富也 - Literally 'greatly wealthy,' used to describe the wealth of the family.
  • 海寇之乱 - Refers to the turmoil caused by sea bandits or pirates during the early Qing dynasty.
  • 移家金坛 - Means 'moved their family to Jintan,' indicating a relocation due to pirate troubles.
  • 行李甚富 - Describes the trapper's possessions as very rich or abundant.
  • 愿假尊屋暂住 - A polite way of asking to borrow a house temporarily.
  • 知非常人 - Means 'knew he was not an ordinary person,' suggesting the host's intuition about the trapper's unusual nature.
  • 分一宅居之 - Means 'gave him a separate house to live in,' indicating the host's hospitality.
  • 暇日过与闲话 - Means 'on a leisure day, visited him for casual conversation,' describing a friendly interaction.
  • 心性《语录》中语 - Refers to phrases from the 'Lüshi Chunqiu' (Master Lü's Spring and Autumn Annals), a collection of philosophical and ethical teachings.
  • 遇其子孙奴仆甚严 - Means 'treated his descendants and servants very strictly,' highlighting the trapper's strict demeanor.
  • 言笑不苟 - Means 'spoke and laughed without being frivolous,' indicating a serious and dignified attitude.
  • 遽抱之 - Means 'suddenly hugged him,' describing the sudden and unexpected action of the maid.
  • 白于胡翁 - Means 'reported to the trapper's father,' indicating the maid's action of informing the trapper.
  • 汝勿怒，吾将杖之 - Means 'don't be angry, I will punish him,' a statement made by the trapper to calm the maid.
  • 明曰日将午 - Means 'the next day at noon,' specifying the time when the trapper's household disappeared.
  • 累叩不应 - Means 'knocked repeatedly but got no response,' describing the unsuccessful attempts to communicate with the trapper's household.
  • 遣人逾墙开门阅之 - Means 'sent someone to climb over the wall and open the door to check,' describing the host's efforts to investigate the empty house.
  • 宅内一无所有 - Means 'the house was completely empty,' indicating the sudden disappearance of the trapper's household.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐道学 - A term that combines the image of a fox (often associated with trickery or supernatural beings in Chinese folklore) with the concept of a scholar, creating a satirical figure.
  • 海寇之乱 - Refers to the period of pirate activity along the coast during the early Qing dynasty, which often disrupted coastal communities.
  • 金坛 - A place name, referring to a city in Jiangsu Province, China.
  • 《黄庭》、《道德》等经 - Refers to the 'Huangting Jing' (Yellow Court Scripture) and the 'Daode Jing' (Tao Te Ching), important texts in Daoism.
  • 心性《语录》中语 - Refers to the 'Lüshi Chunqiu' (Master Lü's Spring and Autumn Annals), a collection of philosophical and ethical teachings attributed to Lü Buwei, a prominent statesman and philosopher of the Warring States period.
  • 租资 - Refers to rental fees, indicating a financial transaction.

Proper Nouns

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 绕棺哀鸣 - Literal phrase meaning 'circled the coffin and mourned,' describing the cats' behavior after the mistress's death.
  • 喂以鱼飧，流泪不食，饿三日，竟同死 - Literal phrase meaning 'fed with fish, they wept and refused to eat, starving for three days until they all died,' detailing the cats' reaction to the mistress's death.
  • 解饷入都，路过山东，宿古庙中 - Literal phrase meaning 'delivering money to the capital, passing through Shandong, and staying in an ancient temple,' setting the scene for the second story.
  • 林恃勇，夜张灯烛，坐以待之 - Literal phrase meaning 'Lin relied on his courage, lit lamps and candles at night, and sat waiting for it,' describing the protagonist's boldness in facing the supernatural.
  • 半夜后橐橐有声，一红衣女踏梯上 - Literal phrase meaning 'after midnight, there was a tapping sound, and a red-cloaked woman climbed the stairs,' introducing the supernatural entity.

  • 被发目，向前扑林 - Literal phrase meaning 'with disheveled hair and wild eyes, she rushed forward to attack Lin,' describing the woman's appearance and attack.
  • 林取几掷之，女侧身避几，而以手来牵 - Literal phrase meaning 'Lin picked up a table and threw it at her, and she dodged it but reached out to pull him,' showing the struggle between Lin and the woman.
  • 林握其手，冷硬如铁 - Literal phrase meaning 'Lin grabbed her hand, which was cold and hard like iron,' describing the woman's supernatural nature.
  • 女被握，不能动。乃以口吹林，臭气难耐 - Literal phrase meaning 'the woman was held and could not move. Then she blew in Lin's face, and the stench was unbearable,' detailing the woman's supernatural attack.

  • 格斗良久，至鸡鸣时，女身倒地，乃僵尸也 - Literal phrase meaning 'after a long struggle, at the crow of the rooster, the woman fell to the ground, and she was a zombie,' concluding the battle and revealing the woman's true nature.

  • 虾蟆教书蚁排阵 - Literal phrase meaning 'frogs teaching ants to arrange themselves in formation,' used to describe a performance by beggars.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 且与缠足 - '缠足' refers to the practice of foot binding, which was a custom among women in China, particularly during the Ming and Qing dynasties. The phrase suggests that the individual involved himself in this practice, which is unusual for a man.

  • 何必伤人闺阃 - '闺阃' refers to the inner quarters of a woman's home, symbolizing her private life. The phrase suggests that the individual did not want to harm anyone's private life.
  • 讯以三木 - '三木' refers to wooden restraints used in ancient Chinese punishments, often used to bind the hands and feet. This indicates that the individual was subjected to physical punishment.
  • 始供吐某某 - '始供吐' means 'began to confess,' and '某某' refers to the names of the individuals involved. This indicates that the individual finally confessed to his crimes.
  • 抚军欲拟长流 - '抚军' refers to a military governor, and '拟长流' means 'proposed a long-term exile.' This indicates that the military governor wanted to exile the individual for a long time.
  • 臬使争以为妖人 - '臬使' refers to a provincial governor, and '争以为妖人' means 'argued that he was a demon.' This indicates that the provincial governor argued that the individual was a demon.

  • 我享人间未有之乐 - '人间未有之乐' means 'unheard-of joy in the world,' suggesting that the individual experienced something extraordinary.
  • 后三年，讯我者在此矣 - '后三年' means 'three years later,' and '讯我者在此矣' means 'the person who will interrogate me will be here.' This indicates that the individual predicted that someone would interrogate him three years later.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 缠足 - Foot binding was a practice in China that lasted from the Song Dynasty to the early 20th century. It was a symbol of beauty and status for women but also a form of physical and social control.
  • 绣师 - Embroidery was a highly respected art form in China, often associated with women. The individual becoming a teacher of embroidery suggests a shift in his social role.
  • 三木 - Wooden restraints were a form of physical punishment in ancient China, often used to bind the hands and feet of criminals.
  • 抚军 - Military governors were high-ranking officials in ancient China, responsible for both military and civil administration in their provinces.
  • 臬使 - Provincial governors were responsible for judicial and administrative matters in their provinces. They often had significant power and influence.

  • 忽轉東風 - Means 'suddenly the wind turned east,' describing a change in weather.
  • 天低若蓋 - Means 'the sky seemed to lower like a lid,' evoking a sense of impending doom.
  • 將壓人頭 - Means 'about to press down on people's heads,' further emphasizing the oppressive atmosphere.
  • 見黑龙在雲中拖尾取水 - Means 'they saw a black dragon dragging its tail to fetch water,' describing the dragon's actions.
  • 數卷后 - Means 'after several turns,' indicating the duration of the dragon's actions.
  • 洪澤湖水低三丈 - Means 'the water level of Hongze Lake dropped by three zhang,' describing the effect of the dragon's actions.
  • 人心大安 - Means 'people's hearts were greatly reassured,' indicating the relief felt by the people.
  • 龍之鱗甲金光四射 - Means 'the dragon's scales shone with golden light,' describing the dragon's appearance.
  • 惟頭身則不可見 - Means 'only the head and body were not visible,' indicating the dragon's partial visibility.
  • 此石埭縣教官沈公雨潭所目擊 - Means 'this was witnessed by Mr. Shen Yutan, the county schoolmaster of Shidai,' indicating the source of the account.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 何以死于此 - A question meaning 'Why did you die here?' or 'What caused you to die here?' The phrase is a standard classical way of inquiring about the cause of death.
  • 前太仓刺史赵酉远亲也 - 'Was a close relative of the former magistrate of Taichang, Zhao You.' The phrase '前太仓刺史' refers to a former official who once held the title of magistrate (刺史) in Taichang (太仓), and '远亲' means 'distant relative' or 'relative from afar.'
  • 赍粮而来 - 'Came bearing provisions.' '赍' means to carry or bring with one, and '粮' is grain or food supplies.
  • 穷馁怨死 - 'Died of hunger and resentment.' '穷' means poverty or destitution, '馁' is hunger, and '怨' is resentment or grievance.
  • 缠赵 - 'To bother Zhao' or 'to pursue Zhao.' In the context, it refers to the ghost trying to haunt or follow Zhao.
  • 幕友与汝宁有冤乎 - 'Does the associate have a grudge against you?' '幕友' is a literary term for a scholar or advisor in an official’s entourage, often used in the context of government offices or private retinues.
  • 鬼无路引不能出境 - 'A ghost without a travel permit cannot leave the territory.' '路引' is a travel permit or official document used in ancient China for travel, especially for officials or people moving between regions.
  • 庶几惊动主人 - 'In hopes of startling the master.' '庶几' is a polite expression meaning 'perhaps' or 'in hopes that.'
  • 哀怜幕友 - 'Pity the associate.' '哀怜' is to feel pity or compassion.
  • 放莫容非魂归故乡 - 'Let the soul of Mo Rongfei return to his homeland.' '放' here is used in the sense of allowing or releasing.
  • 病不医而愈 - 'The illness was cured without treatment.' This is a paradoxical expression that likely means the illness was miraculously cured by the act of helping Mo Rongfei.
  • 石揆、谛晖二僧 - 'The two monks Shi Kui and Di Hui.' '二僧' is 'two monks.'
  • 南能教也 - 'Followers of the Southern School (of Chan Buddhism).' '南能' refers to the Southern Patriarch of Chan Buddhism, Master Huineng, who founded the Southern School, contrasting with the Northern School of Shenxiu.
  • 参禅 - 'To meditate in the Chan Buddhist tradition.' '参' is to investigate or contemplate, and '禅' is Chan (Zen) Buddhism.
  • 持戒 - 'To uphold precepts.' This is a key practice in Buddhist monastic life.
  • 各不相下 - 'Each did not yield to the other.' This means they were rivals or equal in status and did not concede.
  • 避去 - 'To flee or retreat.' Often used to describe monks or religious figures leaving a place.
  • 隐云栖最僻处 - 'Retreated to the most secluded part of Yunqi.' '云栖' is a place, likely referring to a monastery or retreat location.
  • 为灵隐长老 - 'Became the abbot of Lingyin.' '长老' is a title for a senior monk or abbot.
  • 万历孝廉 - 'A scholar-official from the Wanli period.' '孝廉' was a title given to candidates selected through the imperial examination system, often used to denote someone of moral integrity and scholarly ability.
  • 口若悬河 - A common idiom meaning 'to speak fluently and eloquently,' like a river hanging from the sky.
  • 兰若之会 - 'The gathering at the monastery.' '兰若' is a term for a monastery or a quiet retreat, often used in Buddhist contexts.
  • 震动一时 - 'Shook the world at the time' or 'caused a great stir in its day.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有沈氏儿丧父母 - Narrative about a young boy from the Shen family who lost his parents.
  • 为人佣工 - Worked as a laborer for others, indicating his early life circumstances.
  • 随施主入寺 - Followed a benefactor into the temple, suggesting a connection to a religious setting.
  • 石揆见之大惊 - Shi Kui saw him and was greatly surprised, indicating a strong reaction.
  • 愿乞此儿为弟子 - Wished to adopt this child as a disciple, showing Shi Kui's desire to take the boy under his wing.
  • 施主许之 - The benefactor agreed, indicating consent.
  • 儿方七岁 - The child was just seven years old, specifying his age.
  • 即为延师教读 - Immediately hired a teacher to educate him, showing Shi Kui's commitment to the boy's education.
  • 儿欲肉食，即与之肉 - If the child wanted meat, it was given to him, indicating indulgence.
  • 儿欲衣绣，即衣之绣 - If the child wanted embroidered clothes, he was given them, further showing indulgence.
  • 不削发也 - He did not shave his head, indicating that the boy was not fully initiated into monastic life.
  • 儿亦聪颖，通举子业 - The child was also intelligent and proficient in the studies required for the imperial examinations, highlighting his academic abilities.
  • 年将冠矣 - He was about to reach adulthood, indicating his age.

  • 令儿应考，取名近思 - Ordered the child to take the exam and named him Jinsi, showing the official's involvement.
  • 遂取中府学第三名 - He was ranked third in the prefectural school, indicating his success.
  • 命跪佛前剃其发 - Ordered him to kneel before the Buddha and shave his head, showing a change in status.
  • 披以袈裟 - Dressed him in a monk's robe, further indicating his new status.
  • 同学诸生闻之大怒 - The fellow students heard of it and were greatly angry, indicating a strong reaction.
  • 连名数百人上控巡抚学院 - Joined names and petitioned the governor and the school superintendent, showing a collective action.
  • 有项霜泉者，仁和学霜也 - There was Xiang Shuangquan, the head of the Renshou school, introducing a key figure.
  • 率家僮数十篡取近思 - Led dozens of servants to forcibly take Jinsi, showing a dramatic action.
  • 为假辫以饰之 - Made a fake queue to cover his head, indicating an attempt to disguise him.
  • 即以己妹配之 - Immediately married his own sister to him, showing a personal connection.
  • 置酒作乐，聚三学弟子员赋《催妆诗》作贺 - Held a banquet and made music, gathering students from three schools to compose a *Cui Zhuang* poem to congratulate, indicating a celebratory event.
  • 诸大府虽与石揆交，而众怒难犯 - The officials, though they were friends with Shi Kui, found it difficult to face the public anger, showing a conflict between personal relationships and public pressure.
  • 不得已，准诸生所控 - Reluctantly, they allowed the students' petition, indicating a compromise.
  • 许近思蓄发为儒 - Allowed Jinsi to keep his hair and be a Confucian scholar, showing the resolution.
  • 诸生犹不服，各汹汹然 - The students were still不服, each looking very angry, indicating ongoing dissatisfaction.
  • 欲焚灵隐寺殴石揆 - Wanted to burn Lingyin Temple and beat Shi Kui, showing the intensity of their anger.
  • 大府不得已，取石揆两侍者，各笞十五 - The officials, unable to do otherwise, took two of Shi Kui's attendants and each beat them fifteen times, showing a punitive action.
  • 群忿始息 - The public anger finally subsided, indicating the end of the conflict.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 后一月 - Temporal marker indicating the passage of time.
  • 命侍者撞钟鼓召集合寺僧 - Compound verb phrase meaning 'ordered the attendants to strike the bell and drum to summon and gather the monks of the temple.'
  • 各持香一炷礼佛毕 - Each held one stick of incense and paid homage to the Buddha, indicating a ritualistic act of worship.
  • 此予负谛晖之报也 - Complex sentence meaning 'This is my retribution for wronging Dihui,' where '负' implies wrongdoing or debt, and '报' is retribution or karmic consequence.
  • 灵隐本谛晖所住地 - 'Lingyin was originally the place where Dihui dwelled,' with '所住地' indicating the original residence.
  • 一念争胜之心 - 'A single thought of striving for superiority,' a phrase indicating a competitive or prideful mindset.
  • 念己身灭度后 - 'Thinking of myself after my own parinirvana,' where '灭度' refers to the state of Nirvana or death of an enlightened being.
  • 非有大福分人，不能撑持此地 - 'Only someone with great merit and fortune can uphold this place,' with '福分' meaning karmic fortune or merit.
  • 沈氏儿风骨严整 - 'The son of the Shen family has a noble and upright spirit,' with '风骨' indicating moral integrity and dignity.
  • 在人间为一品官，在佛家为罗汉身 - 'In the world, he is a first-rank official; in the Buddhist tradition, he is a bodhisattva or arhat,' showing dual status.
  • 余见而倾心，欲以此坐与之 - 'I saw him and was deeply moved, wishing to give this seat to him,' where '此坐' refers to the position or seat of authority.
  • 又一念争胜，欲使佛法胜于孔子 - 'Another thought of superiority, wishing to make Buddhism surpass Confucianism,' indicating a desire to elevate one tradition above another.
  • 先使入学，以继我孝廉出身之衣钵 - 'First, I made him enter school, to continue my legacy as a scholar-official,' with '孝廉' being a Confucian scholar-official and '衣钵' meaning legacy or spiritual succession.
  • 贪嗔未灭之客气 - 'The residual arrogance of unextinguished greed and anger,' where '客气' can mean arrogance or pride.
  • 侍儿受杖，为辱已甚 - 'The attendant has been beaten with the staff; this is a great humiliation,' indicating a punishment.
  • 尚何面目坐方丈乎 - 'How can I face the people and sit in the abbot’s seat?' with '方丈' being the abbot’s seat or the head monk’s position.
  • 儒家之改过，即佛家之忏悔也 - 'The Confucian act of correcting one's mistakes is equivalent to the Buddhist act of repentance,' showing a parallel between the two traditions.
  • 吾将赴释梵天王处忏悔百年 - 'I will go to the realm of the Buddha and King of the Gods to repent for a hundred years,' with '释梵天王' referring to the Buddha and the King of the Gods (Brahma).
  • 诸弟子速持我禅杖一枝，白玉钵盂一个、紫衣袈裟一袭往迎谛晖，为我补过 - Complex imperative: 'All disciples, quickly take one of my staffs, one white jade bowl, and one set of purple robe and袈裟 to go and welcome Dihui, to make amends for me.'
  • 群僧合掌跪泣曰 - 'The monks joined their palms, knelt, and wept, saying,' a formal expression of mourning and submission.
  • 谛晖逃出已三十年，音耗寂然 - 'Dihui has fled for thirty years, and there has been no word from him,' indicating his disappearance.
  • 现在云栖第几山第几寺，户外有松一株、井一口 - 'He is now in the cloud-dwelling (cloud-hermit) temple at the [X]th mountain and [X]th temple, where there is one pine tree and one well outside,' with '云栖' possibly referring to a specific location or monastic site.
  • 趺坐而逝，鼻垂玉柱二尺许 - 'He sat in the lotus position and passed away, with a two-foot-long jade column hanging from his nose,' a supernatural or symbolic description of his death.
  • 群僧如其言，果得谛晖 - 'The monks followed his words, and indeed found Dihui,' indicating the successful fulfillment of the instructions.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 石揆 - A name, possibly of a Buddhist monk or abbot who is the subject of the narrative.
  • 谛晖 - A name, likely a fellow monk or a spiritual figure who was wronged by 石揆.
  • 灵隐 - Refers to Lingyin Temple (灵隐寺) in Hangzhou, one of the most famous and ancient Buddhist temples in China.
  • 禅杖 - A Buddhist staff, used by monks as a symbol of authority and spiritual discipline.
  • 白玉钵盂 - A white jade bowl, a sacred object used for holding offerings or food during meditation and rituals.
  • 紫衣袈裟 - A purple robe and monastic robe (袈裟), indicating the formal attire of a Buddhist monk.
  • 方丈 - The abbot’s seat, or the position of the head monk in a monastery.
  • 儒家之改过，即佛家之忏悔也 - A comparative statement between Confucian and Buddhist ethics, indicating a fusion or parallel between the two traditions.
  • 释梵天王 - A composite term combining Buddha (释) and Brahma (梵天王), the King of the Gods in Hindu and Buddhist cosmology.
  • 云栖 - Possibly refers to Cloud-Dwelling Temple (云栖寺), a famous monastery in Hangzhou, or a symbolic place of spiritual retreat.
  • 趺坐而逝 - 'Sitting in the lotus position and passing away,' a Buddhist expression for a peaceful and enlightened death.
  • 鼻垂玉柱二尺许 - A symbolic or supernatural description, possibly indicating the monk’s spiritual attainment or transformation.

  • 身姓符，本籍河南，寄居于此 - My name is Fu. I am originally from Henan but am living here temporarily.
  • 孀居无子，只生一女名宜春 - I am a widow with no sons, and I only have a daughter named Yichun.

  • 如先生者正是合式 - Someone like you is exactly suitable.
  • 未知手段何如 - But I am unsure of your skills.

  • 相配 - Refers to marriage, indicating a social and familial arrangement.
  • 合式 - Refers to suitability, often used in the context of marriage or other social arrangements.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刘伯温 - Refers to Liu Bowen (1311–1375), a renowned military strategist and advisor to the first emperor of the Ming dynasty, Zhu Yuanzhang. He is known for his strategic acumen and contributions to the founding of the Ming dynasty.

  • 城隍神 - Refers to the city god, a deity believed to protect the city and its inhabitants. Officials traditionally paid homage to the city god upon taking office.
  • 乾隆 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Qianlong (1735–1796), the sixth emperor of the Qing dynasty, known for his long reign and cultural achievements.

  • 牲牢祭奠 - Refers to the preparation of sacrificial offerings, typically involving animals, to appease the spirits or deities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 南唐李煜 - Li Yu of Southern Tang. Li Yu (937–978) was the last emperor of the Southern Tang dynasty, known for his poetry and tragic end.
  • 吴越王 - The King of Wuyue. This refers to the kingdom of Wuyue, which existed during the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period (907–979). Its ruler was Qian Liu, and later his descendants.
  • 乾隆初年 - The early years of the Qianlong reign. Emperor Qianlong (1711–1799) ruled the Qing dynasty from 1736 to 1795.
  • 金娥墩 - A specific location in Wuxi, Jiangsu Province. The name suggests a historical or memorial site.
  • 篆四字 - Four characters in seal script. Seal script (zhuanshu) is an ancient Chinese script used in official seals and inscriptions.
  • 庐山 - Mount Lu, a famous mountain in Jiangxi Province, known for its Daoist and Buddhist monasteries and as a place of spiritual retreat.
  • 炼形分身之法 - The method of refining one's form and dividing into multiple bodies. This is a Daoist alchemical and magical concept, referring to the ability to create multiple physical forms or to transcend the physical body.
  • 得道 - To attain Dao or to achieve spiritual transcendence. This is a central concept in Daoism.
  • 广信府 - Guangxin Prefecture, an old administrative division in Jiangxi Province, now part of Yiwu or Shangrao.

The character '胃' (wèi) is a variant of '胃' (wèi), which can mean 'stomach' or 'gut,' but in this context, it likely refers to a type of needle or pricking sensation.

  • 达机自服之 - Means 'Da Ji put it on himself.' '自服' (zì fú) means 'to put on oneself.'

  • The sentence '忽有牛突入铅厂' is elliptical and could be interpreted as 'Suddenly, a cow rushed into the lead factory.'

  • The sentence '屠人执绳追至' is elliptical and could be interpreted as 'The butcher grabbed the rope and chased after it.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 活死人墓道人江文谷于洗心池旁培小阜，叠石塞牖，跌坐于中，嘱其徒云：“每日向牖呼我，应则已，水应则入收遗蜕。” This sentence describes a complex sequence of actions and instructions, where Jiang Wengu, a hermit, is preparing a small mound near the Washin Pool, blocking the windows with stones, and sitting in a meditative posture. He instructs his disciples to call out to him each day, and if he responds, they should continue; if the water responds, they should enter to collect his remains.
  • 忽一日应曰：“可厌，吾去矣！”嗣后不应，启石视之，尸果僵，故称活死人墓。 This sentence describes a sudden change in the hermit's behavior, where he suddenly responds with 'I am tired of this, I am leaving!' and then stops responding. The disciples later find that his body has become stiff, leading to the term 'living-dead person's tomb.'
  • 金公据实奏闻，上怜之，俱钦赐生员。 This sentence describes Jin Deying reporting the incident to the emperor, who shows compassion and grants all the surviving students the status of scholar-officials.
  • 省中府县俱来请安，史公笑谓曰：“设使四大员一时并命，则司道之印，诸公委署，不皆有分乎！” This sentence describes a humorous and philosophical reflection by Shi Yiting, where he imagines what would happen if the four officials had died at the same time, suggesting that the seals and duties would be distributed among the remaining officials.
